source.title: The influence of sleep on fear extinction in trauma-related disorders

confidence_rationale: High-quality review in a specialist venue with direct mechanistic relevance to MECH-124. The paper's core argument -- that sleep disruption in PTSD selectively impairs extinction consolidation while leaving fear traces intact -- is the biological substrate for the MECH-124 failure mode: a consolidation system that sees imbalanced input (harm-trace dominant) produces imbalanced output (fear amplified, extinction/goal traces weakened). Mapping fidelity is strong because the mechanism described (REM fragmentation -> failed extinction consolidation -> chronic fear trace dominance) maps cleanly onto the REE loop. Transfer risk is moderate because the paper concerns clinical PTSD in humans whereas REE instantiates the mechanism computationally.
failure_signatures: Sleep disruption after trauma preferentially impairs extinction memory consolidation, leaving fear traces relatively intact -- producing an asymmetric imbalance.
REM fragmentation during the critical post-trauma window impedes normal fear-extinction consolidation, perpetuating fear trace dominance.
Individuals with PTSD show deficient recall of extinction memories relative to healthy controls, consistent with failed consolidation of safety/extinction content.
Positive feedback loops between stress physiology, REM disruption, and fear trace entrenchment create a self-amplifying cycle.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Sleep disruption may contribute to the etiology of PTSD by interfering with consolidation of fear-extinction memories. REM fragmentation after trauma prevents consolidation of safety learning, leaving fear traces dominant. This produces a self-amplifying cycle: unextinguished fear -> hyperarousal -> worse sleep -> worse extinction consolidation -> stronger fear.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-124 proposes that if harm-trace salience dominates offline replay content, consolidation selectively amplifies harm predictions, progressively contracting the option space. Pace-Schott et al. provide the clinical ground truth: the mechanism is real, operating in humans, and is self-amplifying. The PTSD extinction-failure model is the biological analog of the MECH-124 loop: when consolidation input is harm-dominated, consolidation output strengthens harm relative to extinction/goal traces, not through any bug but through the normal operation of salience-weighted consolidation. For REE: if the residue field is harm-dominant at the onset of the offline phase (MECH-121), z_goal representations will receive proportionally less consolidation support, accelerating goal trace decay. The MECH-094 hypothesis tag is the proposed circuit-level intervention: protecting goal-directed traces from being outcompeted during replay scheduling.
mapping.mapping_caveat: MECH-124 is V4 scope -- REE does not yet implement a sleep/offline phase. The clinical PTSD mechanism involves HPA axis dysregulation, hippocampal volume effects, and multifactor REM disruption that do not map one-to-one onto REE design variables. The key translation is structural (imbalanced consolidation input -> imbalanced output) rather than mechanistic detail-for-detail. The paper also focuses on the aftermath of acute trauma, whereas MECH-124 describes a gradual drift during normal operation.
mapping.source_context: Narrative review of human and animal PTSD literature, focusing on the interaction between REM sleep physiology and fear-extinction consolidation. Clinical and pre-clinical studies reviewed.

confidence_components.notes: Strong source quality: peer-reviewed specialist review, PMC-indexed, covers both clinical and pre-clinical evidence. Mapping fidelity high: the imbalanced consolidation mechanism is structurally identical to MECH-124's proposed loop. Transfer risk moderate: clinical PTSD involves factors absent in REE (HPA dysregulation, discrete trauma event), but the core dynamic (salience-weighted consolidation -> asymmetric strengthening) is domain-general.
